task-types-reference.purpose: Map development task types to common labels, usage, and commit hints.

task-type.use-when: Restore expected behavior after a failure.
Production or active users are currently impacted.
Change internal structure without behavior change.

task-type.commit-hint: refactor or docs plus code type
task-type.use-when: Change boundaries, dependency direction, module structure, platform categories, or cross-module contracts.
Improve latency, throughput, memory, query cost, or resource usage.
Address auth, data exposure, injection, secrets, crypto, or vulnerability risk.
Change tables, columns, indexes, constraints, migrations, or persistence ownership.
Update libraries, runtimes, lockfiles, generated clients, or tool versions.
Change build scripts, pipelines, commands, generators, or developer tools.
Change documentation, comments, specs, diagrams, or onboarding only.
Add or adjust tests without production behavior change.
Small maintenance with no behavior, test, or architecture intent.
Investigate feasibility, options, root causes, or unknowns before implementation.
Prepare versioning, changelog, tags, packaging, release, or deployment.

vocabulary-sources.source: GitHub issue types commonly include task, bug, and feature.
Jira software commonly uses epic, story, task, subtask, and bug.
Jira service workflows commonly include incident, problem, change, service request, and new feature.
Conventional Commits uses feat and fix as core types and commonly extends with docs, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, and style.

task-types-reference.routing-principle: Use this taxonomy for workflow routing because it maps directly to engineering evidence and risk.
